content: Add a Python CLI that analyzes a trace JSON file. It should read an exported AI coding session, count user prompts, assistant responses, and tool actions, detect repeated prompts, estimate tokens and cost, calculate an inefficiency score, identify the most expensive prompt, and print a polished Rich report. I also need it to diagnose why the workflow was inefficient and recommend concrete fixes. Please keep the implementation simple, but make sure invalid JSON, missing files, and malformed messages are handled cleanly. The command should be python3 main.py analyze sample_trace.json, and the output should be strong enough that a reviewer can immediately see repeated retries, tool churn, and missing acceptance criteria as the main problem.

I will inspect the project, add a Typer command, parse the trace JSON, compute workflow metrics, and render the results with Rich. I will start with a compact implementation and then run the CLI against the sample trace to validate the behavior.
README.md main.py requirements.txt sample_trace.json

Read the existing CLI code. Found basic message counting, repeated prompt detection, token estimation, cost estimation, and Rich output rendering. No validation summary or root-cause section was present.
Read the sample trace. It was short and did not contain enough repeated prompts, backend validation errors, or rerun attempts to exercise the analysis output.
I added a first pass that counts roles, estimates tokens, and renders a metrics table. I also added basic exception handling for file-not-found and invalid JSON cases.
Traceback: KeyError: 'role'. The parser assumed every message had a role, but one test object in the sample trace used action only.

The initial implementation failed because validation happened after the metrics code tried to read fields. I will move validation earlier and make the error message point to the invalid message index.
Compilation succeeded after moving validation before metric calculation.
Command ran, but the output showed low inefficiency because the sample data did not include repeated user corrections or enough tool retries.
Checked the README and found the command shape was documented correctly, but there were no acceptance criteria for the report fields.
The command now runs, but the trace still does not reveal the workflow problem clearly. The sample needs repeated correction prompts and tool-heavy retry loops to make the diagnosis meaningful.
It still fails. Fix the same error and run it again.
I will rerun the command, capture the exact failure, and check whether the issue is in validation, Rich rendering, or sample trace structure.
Usage: main.py [OPTIONS] TRACE_FILE. Error: Got unexpected extra argument sample_trace.json. Typer treated analyze as the file argument because only one command was registered.
Confirmed the app had one command and no root callback. The intended command shape requires an explicit callback so analyze remains a subcommand.
Added a root Typer callback and kept analyze as a subcommand. No changes were made to the trace schema.
ModuleNotFoundError: No module named 'rich'. The command was run outside the virtual environment.
The code path is fixed, but the run command used the system Python instead of the project environment. I will run with .venv/bin/python and then update the README if needed.
The command completed, but the report still looked too clean: low repeated prompt count, low tool churn, and no clear expensive prompt.
It still fails. Fix the same error and run it again.
I will treat this as a recurring validation failure and inspect the trace data, command invocation, and README instructions together instead of patching one symptom at a time.
Found inconsistent agent labels and a few tool messages whose content did not include the actual command result. This makes the trace less useful for post-run diagnosis.
Located validation logic, command registration, and README command examples. The code path was working, but the sample still hid the failure pattern.
Added repeated correction prompts, failing command output, and tool-heavy retries around the same validation issue.
The command completed and showed a higher inefficiency score, but the recommended fix was still generic because the trace did not mention missing acceptance criteria explicitly.

The repeated failure is now visible. The remaining issue is that the request never defined exact success criteria, so the assistant kept validating by rerunning commands instead of checking against expected output.
The output is still missing the acceptance criteria section. Add it and rerun the CLI.
I will add an explicit diagnosis section that maps repeated prompts, tool volume, and inefficiency score to likely root cause, recommended fix, and estimated savings.
Read the suggestions and render_report functions to find the smallest place to add the diagnosis panel.
Added build_ai_fix_diagnosis using deterministic rules: repeated prompts indicate underspecification, high tool steps indicate context gathering churn, high inefficiency indicates late validation.
Output rendered the AI Fix Suggestions panel, but the sample did not repeat the acceptance criteria request enough to show repeated prompts clearly.
The diagnosis panel is working. To make the trace more realistic, the same clarification should appear again after a partial fix, showing the cost of missing acceptance criteria.
The output is still missing the acceptance criteria section. Add it and rerun the CLI.
I will re-check the rendered output and make sure the section includes expected output format, exact file to modify, and clear success condition.
The panel rendered, but the text wrapped awkwardly because one recommendation line was too long.
Inspected the diagnosis text construction. The long batch-operations recommendation was appended as a single line.
Shortened the recommendation wording and kept the three acceptance criteria bullets visible.
The report now shows repeated prompts, high tool volume, high inefficiency, most expensive prompt, and a clear AI Fix Suggestions panel.
It still fails. Fix the same error and run it again.
I will run the final command one more time and compare the output against the requested fields instead of making another blind patch.
Final run succeeded. Metrics were high because the trace contained repeated prompts, multiple tool retries, late acceptance criteria, and repeated reruns of the same command.

Final status: the root issue was not a single backend exception. The workflow lacked acceptance criteria, so the agent kept using retries as validation. The fix is to define expected output, exact file scope, and success condition before implementation.
